text: The surgeon begins preperitoneal dissection after reducing the incarcerated omentum, preparing to create a flap starting at least five centimeters cranially from the hernia. The incision is made at the falciform fat and curves outward, aiming for a reproducible approach with minimal risk of perforations.

The surgeon transitions to dissecting the central portion with less fat, using closed scissors to wiggle into the space between the peritoneum and fascia. The burn and cut technique is employed to prevent bleeding. The initial transection is completed, and the dissection of the peritoneum continues, focusing on the fatty tissue. The flap extends to the junction of the peritoneal fat and peritoneum on the contralateral side, completing the superior initial dissection.

The surgeon performs caudal dissection of the peritoneum, ensuring there is no tension to avoid creating holes. The dissection progresses centrally, revealing a layer of fat that facilitates the preperitoneal dissection. The surgeon then reduces two hernia sacs, which appear as a double volcano sign, around the ligaments of the umbilicus.

The surgeon focuses on hemostasis, using electrocautery to proactively manage potential bleeding sites. On the contralateral side, the dissection is easier compared to the ipsilateral side. The surgeon carefully dissects between multiple leaflets of transversalis fascia, aiming to leave as much tissue on the peritoneum as possible to minimize holes. Two distinct layers of tissue are visible, with a red arrow indicating the peritoneum.

The surgeon uses electrocautery to burn the posterior sheath and then pushes away to develop a dissection plane. The broad space created is prepared for the placement of a 15x15 mesh. The suture line for defect closure is shown, demonstrating a standard closure technique.

The surgeon closes the abdominal wall in a vertical orientation using a permanent V-lock number one suture. The suture is sequentially tightened to bring the tissue edges together without trauma. The fascia edges are re-approximated, and the suture is run back on itself to lock it, ensuring that the reverse stitches are placed between existing sutures to minimize hole size.

The surgeon places a Bard Soft Macroporous Polypropylene mesh with the rough side facing anteriorly to adhere to the abdominal wall. The mesh is fixed with sutures at five points: one in each corner and one centrally. The surgeon uses air knots to prevent mesh deformation. For peritoneal flap closure, two six-inch stitches are used, employing a canal stitch superiorly and a baseball stitch inferiorly to demonstrate different techniques.

The surgeon demonstrates the baseball stitch technique, commonly used for tension-free flap closure, with visible exposed sutures. The suture line is tied, and the knot is dunked underneath the tissue.
